title: Dog Ear Flap Swollen with Fluid? A Guide to Aural Hematomas
meta_desc: Is your dog's ear flap swollen or puffy? Learn about aural hematomas, their urgency, and why your vet needs a photo to help triage.
content_html:
What Is It?

If your dog's ear flap feels like a soft, warm, fluid-filled balloon or a "pillow," they likely have an aural hematoma. This occurs when a blood vessel within the ear flap ruptures, causing blood and fluid to pool between the skin and the ear cartilage. It is often the result of vigorous head shaking or scratching caused by an underlying issue like an infection or allergies.

Is it an Emergency?

The urgency level for a swollen ear flap is Medium. While this is not typically a life-threatening emergency, it is a painful condition that requires medical attention. If left untreated, the hematoma can cause permanent scarring and deformity of the ear (often called cauliflower ear). You should aim to see a veterinarian within 24 to 48 hours to provide relief and address the root cause.

Why a Photo Helps Triage

Sharing a photo of your dog's ear helps a veterinary professional assess the size of the swelling and the condition of the skin. It allows them to see if the ear canal is obstructed and helps determine how quickly your pet needs to be seen. A photo also serves as a baseline to monitor if the swelling is expanding rapidly.

faqs.question: Can I drain the fluid at home?
faqs.answer: No, you should never attempt to drain an ear hematoma yourself. Doing so carries a high risk of infection and, without proper surgical or medical management, the ear will likely refill with fluid almost immediately.

faqs.question: What will a vet do for a swollen ear flap?
faqs.answer: A veterinarian will likely drain the fluid under sterile conditions or perform a minor surgery to prevent it from refilling. They will also examine the ear canal to treat any infection that may have caused the head shaking in the first place.

faqs.question: Is my dog in pain?
faqs.answer: Yes, aural hematomas are generally heavy and painful. The pressure from the fluid can cause significant discomfort, which is why your dog may continue to shake their head, potentially making the swelling worse.
